coredata.dc:title: Portable smartphone platform based on tunable chiral fluorescent carbon dots for visual detection of L-Asp and L-Lys

wang, Bin

Wang, Chenzhao
Cui, Shufeng

Sun, Guoying
coredata.dc:description: Amino acids are involved in many physiological processes such as neurotransmitter transmission, protein synthesis and immune regulation, which play an indispensable role in life science. However, accurate monitoring of amino acid metabolism still faces a huge challenging due the lack of robust detection approaches. Herein, three-color emission tunable fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were successfully synthesized by a simple one-step solvothermal treatment using classical precursors. These multi-color CDs exhibited a bright and steady luminescence shifting from blue to red under UV–vis light excitation. Detailed characterization by various precision instruments demonstrated that the unique structure of the prepared polychromatic emission CDs was composed of sp2 hybrid carbon nucleus and part of sp3 hybrid carbon atom. These CDs displayed low cytotoxicity and excellent multi-color cell imaging ability at single wavelength excitation. Moreover, the chiral structure on the surface of the red emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) was well preserved at temperatures far below the melting point of the chiral precursor. Especially, the chiral red emissive CDs (R-L-CDs) as sensitive and selective fluorescent probe could quickly identify L-Aspartic acid (L-Asp) and L-Lysine (L-Lys) in water and biological samples. Additionally, smart phone color recognizer application could realize more accurate visual detection to compensate the defect that the naked eye did not distinguish subtle colors. Therefore, the multi-excitation mode could be effectively shield background interference from complex samples by introducing a self-calibrating reference signal, ensuring accurate and reliable quantitative information, endowing R-L-CDs as a biosensor with far-reaching prospects for further application by binding target analytes.

One-step preparation of multicolor CDs and R-L-CDs as a portable smartphone platform for visual detection of L-Asp and L-Lys.

head.ce:title: Portable smartphone platform based on tunable chiral fluorescent carbon dots for visual detection of L-Asp and L-Lys

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Amino acids are involved in many physiological processes such as neurotransmitter transmission, protein synthesis and immune regulation, which play an indispensable role in life science. However, accurate monitoring of amino acid metabolism still faces a huge challenging due the lack of robust detection approaches. Herein, three-color emission tunable fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were successfully synthesized by a simple one-step solvothermal treatment using classical precursors. These multi-color CDs exhibited a bright and steady luminescence shifting from blue to red under UV–vis light excitation. Detailed characterization by various precision instruments demonstrated that the unique structure of the prepared polychromatic emission CDs was composed of sp2 hybrid carbon nucleus and part of sp3 hybrid carbon atom. These CDs displayed low cytotoxicity and excellent multi-color cell imaging ability at single wavelength excitation. Moreover, the chiral structure on the surface of the red emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) was well preserved at temperatures far below the melting point of the chiral precursor. Especially, the chiral red emissive CDs (R-L-CDs) as sensitive and selective fluorescent probe could quickly identify L-Aspartic acid (L-Asp) and L-Lysine (L-Lys) in water and biological samples. Additionally, smart phone color recognizer application could realize more accurate visual detection to compensate the defect that the naked eye did not distinguish subtle colors. Therefore, the multi-excitation mode could be effectively shield background interference from complex samples by introducing a self-calibrating reference signal, ensuring accurate and reliable quantitative information, endowing R-L-CDs as a biosensor with far-reaching prospects for further application by binding target analytes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chirality is an inherent and important characteristic of nature and plays a pivotal function in the origin and evolution of life [1,2] Generally, quite a few molecules are chiral including amino acids, proteins, polysaccharides and enzymes and even many artificially manufactured molecules [3,4]. Therefore, the chirality characteristic is widely applied in chiral recognition, chiral separation and chiral catalysis [4-6]. Chiral nanomaterials such as chiral gold nanoclusters, chiral noble metal nanoparticles, semiconductor quantum dots and chiral related materials have mushroomed in recent 20 years [7]. However, the high cost and toxicity of metal-containing nanoparticles may prevent large-scale commercialization of chiral nanoparticles [8]. Thus, it is urgent to develop new chiral materials with excellent biocompatibility. Among them, carbon dots, especially chiral CDs (Ch-CDs) have received the great attention for their outstanding properties such as superior steric configuration and biological activity, excellent photostability, outstanding biocompatibility, flexible surface modification and tunable emission [9-14]. Great progress over the past few years has been achieved in preparation of approaches, applications (including biosensing, therapeutic diagnostics, drug delivery, bioimaging, optoelectronic devices and energy storage) and mechanism research of CDs [2,15-22]. Consequently, Ch-CDs are expected to play a great potential in chiral recognition, chiral catalysis, biomedicine, environmental monitoring, food analysis and other aspects [4,6,10,12,13,16,23]. So far, some breakthroughs have been obtained in the preparation and application of chiral carbon (Ch-CDs). For example, Kang and his colleagues established that Ch-CDs at 405 nm with the opposite optical rotation to the precursor and the chirality could be regulated by controlling the reaction time [9]. Copur and his colleagues constructed an L-cysteine-functionalized CDs with the emission wavelength at 420 nm for selective detection of L-lysine isomers [14]. Li and his colleagues developed N, S-Ch-CDs with the emission wavelength at 510 nm and manifested the different influences of Ch-CDs on cell energy metabolism for the first time [1]. Lu and his colleagues constructed facile solvent-controlled Ch-CDs with the emission peak at 602 nm for full-color and white circularly polarized luminescence in the first time [15]. Recently, although some encouraging progress has been made in the preparation of Ch-CDs, there are still few reports on red-emitting and wavelength-tunable Ch-CDs due to the lack of further in-depth investigates of the fluorescence properties and chirality of CDs [4,15]. Most of the reported CDs are concentrated in the UV–vis region with blue-green fluorescence, which greatly restricts the further application of CDs in bioimaging due to the strong autofluorescence interference of the biological [4,19-21,24]. On the contrary, red luminescent CDs (>600 nm) have attractive advantages such as strong light penetration depth and low tissue damage, especially in the biomedical field [11,25]. Thus, the development of red emissive chiral carbon dots (R-L-CDs) with long wavelength emission in a facile manner is encouraged, which is still a long revolutionary process of chemists, especially in biosensing and bioimaging [8,11].

L-Aspartic acid (L-Asp), as one of the endogenous amino acids required for human growth, is involved in the synthesis of glutamate, mammalian metabolism, diagnosis of myocardial function and neurotransmitter transmission [18,26]. Unfortunately, the excessive L-Asp can directly lead to liver damage and reduce liver detoxifunction [18,27]. A recent study has demonstrated that certain level of L-Asp in cells is necessary for cell respiration and proliferation, and abnormal level of L-Asp can be applied as a basis for early diagnosis of tumors. L-Lysine (L-Lys) is the essential basic amino acid and can promote human growth and development, improve immunity, but also can cross the blood–brain barrier and enter the brain tissue to provide a necessary energy source for nerve cell repair and normal physiological activities [29-31]. The high concentration of L-Lys in plasma and urine is an indicator of congenital metabolic disorders. Thus, the sensitive identification of L-Asp and L-Lys is of great significance for early tumor diagnosis and prevention of metabolic diseases [3]. Currently, many techniques for amino acid analysis have been extensively developed such as capillary electrophoresis [33], electrochemical voltammetry high-performance liquid chromatography [34], mass spectrometry and fluorescence analysis manner [35]. However, the shortcomings of detection methods including high cost, cumbersome process and time consumption, restrict their wide application in the field of sensing [27]. Encouragingly, fluorescent probes are considered ideal candidates for identifying amino acids, metal ions and harmful substances due to their high sensitivity, specific selectivity, stable photochemical properties and excellent biocompatibility. Therefore, it is of grand importance to construct a simple and rapid fluorescent analysis approach for the detection of L-Asp and L-Lys.

In this work, three types of CDs with tunable photoluminescence were synthesized via one-step solvothermal method using citric acid as precursors (Scheme 1 ). The long wavelength emission was related with enhance of sp2 conjugate domain and the degree of surface oxidation. The completely purified CDs exhibited a unique luminescence from blue to red and the quantum yield (QY) in aqueous solution ranged from 29.17 % to 6.83 %. Importantly, the R-L-CDs as a selective fluorescent probe could be applied to detect L-Asp and L-Lys in aqueous solution with the detection limit of 80 nM and 55.60 nM, respectively. Interestingly, the smartphone color recognizer application can realize more accurate visual detection to compensate for the defect that the naked eye cannot distinguish subtle colors. Therefore, the R-L-CDs displayed outstanding potential in the early diagnosis of liver injury and hyperlysinemia for practical clinical medicine.

The achiral CDs, including blue emissive CDs (B-CDs) and yellow emissive CDs (Y-CDs), were prepared by one-pot solvothermal method of heating CA and urea in water and DMF solution, respectively (Fig. 1 ) [13,21]. Based on this, the chiral red emissive CDs (R-L-CDs and R-D-CDs) were successfully prepared after the introduction of L-isoleucine (L-Lle)/D-isoleucine (D-Lle) [4]. The chiral structure of R-L-CDs and R-D-CDs was well preserved at temperatures far below the melting point of isoleucine (284 °C). Upon the mass ratio of citric acid and L-Lle of 1:1, the optimal reaction temperature of 180 °C and the reaction time of 6 h, these R-L-CDs possessed the strongest fluorescence (Fig. S1a-S1c). Moreover, the difference between R-L-CDs and R-D-CDs was obvious only in their chiral optical properties, so most of the characterizations described below used R-L-CDs as an example. Ultimately, the surface charge engineering of R-L-CDs was achieved with sodium hydroxide treatment to reduce the overlap between fluorescence emission and absorption [20,36].

The different natures of the blue to red emissions were investigated by the UV–vis absorption and fluorescent emission spectra of the three CDs [21]. These CDs in aqueous solution exhibited different colors under sunlight and tunable photoluminescence (PL) emission from blue to red under 365 nm UV [15,24]. As described in Fig. 2 a-2c, the UV–vis absorption spectra exhibited analogous absorption at 200 nm and 240 nm, corresponding to the π → π* transitions of C=C bonds in aromatic sp2 domains [4]. However, the absorption spectra of B-CDs, Y-CDs and R-L-CDs displayed distinct wide absorption bands at ultraviolet region of 300–360 nm, 400–500 nm and 520–630 nm, which suggested that the transitions of their surface states were different [19]. The strong absorption of long wavelength CDs could be attributed to the n → π* transition of the C=N and C=O bonds, which was characterized by absorption red shift as the dielectric constant of the solvent decreased [37]. The dielectric constant of water (78.36 F/m) was significantly higher than that of DMF (36.70 F/m), which supported the n → π* transition property of R-L-CDs [19,37,38]. As illustrated in Fig. 2d-2f, these CDs displayed excitation dependent and excitation independent luminescence characteristics and the maximum emission peaks located at 440 nm, 530 nm and 620 nm, respectively [18]. The absolute quantum yields (QY) of the three CDs measured using integrating spheres at the optimal excitation wavelength in the ultrapure water were 29.17 %, 7.78 % and 6.80 %, respectively (Table S1). Moreover, the time-resolved fluorescence (TRPL) decay curves of B-CDs, Y-CDs and R-L-CDs revealed the dynamic process of electron inactivation through radiation and non-radiation pathways from the excited state to the ground state [39]. As illustrated in Fig. 2g and Table S2, the curves of B-CDs and Y-CDs were fitted by three-exponential decay with a lifetime of 3.53 ns (τ1), 7.05 ns (τ2) and 14.11 ns (τ3) for B-CDs at 370 nm excitation, and 1.74 ns (τ1), 3.48 ns (τ2) and 6.97 ns (τ3) for Y-CDs at 455 nm excitation, which indicated the multiple fluorescent center of B-CDs and Y-CDs. And the decrease of longer time component (τ1) demonstrated gradually enhanced density of traps in these CDs, which was similar with the descending trend of average fluorescence lifetime. Subsequently, the slower component (τ2) could be attributed to non-trap-assisted chargecarrier recombination pathways, which indicated that the surface functional groups between the two samples were similar [40]. For the R-L-CDs, the TRPL decay profiles were fitted with a double exponential function with the lifetimes of 0.13 ns (38.75 %) and 1.38 ns (61.25 %) at 590 nm excitation, indicating that R-L-CDs underwent a co-luminescence process of carbon nuclei and surface states [4,11,19,32]. Circular dichroism (CD) was a crucial technique for checking the absolute chirality of molecules. The chiral features of R-CDs were successfully confirmed by electronic circular dichroism (ECD) [6,7,13]. Interestingly, L-Lle and D-Lle resulted in the generation of R-L-CDs and R-D-CDs with corresponding mirror image CD scans as displayed in Fig. 2h. L-Lle and D-Lle as precursors possessed a strong ECD signal at +200 nm and −200 nm, respectively (Fig. S2), while the CDs signal extended from 200 nm to 300 nm (Fig. 2h), which demonstrated that the precursor experienced carbonization and polymerization [4,8,13]. Obviously, the CD signal of the R-L/D-CDs near 200–205 nm was inherited from the intrinsic chiral chromophore of the L-Lle/D-Lle. And the CD signals from 205 to 300 nm could be associated with the π → π* transitions of derives from the aromatic superficial groups [4,13]. Moreover, the g factor was calculated to be 0.0044 according to the formula (gabs=

≤ 2), which is consistent with the value of gabs for most materials between 10−4 and 10−2 [2]. Therefore, these results revealed that the chiral and achiral precursors were specifically combined during the solvothermal process, and the chirality of R-L/D-CDs derived from the structure of the product itself. Finally, the fluorescence results corresponded to the International Commission on Illumination (CIE) color coordinates (0.58, 0.42) (Fig. 2i), which suggested the red emission of R-L-CDs [16].

The morphology and size of the three CDs were characterized by TEM and AFM. B-CDs, Y-CDs and R-L-CDs were spherical and uniformly distributed with the average sizes of 11.21 ± 0.82 nm, 8.01 ± 0.26 nm and 15.32 ± 0.64 nm, respectively (Fig. 3 a-3c), which indicated that the particle size distribution of these samples was so broad that their fluorescence differences could not be clearly attributed to differences in particle size [36]. The HR-TEM images of these CDs did not display obvious lattice fringes (inset. 3a-3c) [20,21]. Moreover, the AFM profile exhibited that the thickness of the CDs samples was about 1.50 nm, corresponding to 3 to 5 layers of graphene-like (Fig. 3d-3f) [36]. Therefore, the TEM and AFM data demonstrated that the red-shift of fluorescence emission from B-CDs to R-L-CDs was not caused by quantum size effects [21,36]. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns and Raman spectra were applied to investigate the crystalline state of these CDs. As illustrated in Fig. 3g, the B-CDs displayed a relatively weak and broad diffraction peak at 24.2° matching to the (002) plane of the graphite structure, which implied the partial graphitization and larger size of the B-CDs [5,41]. This phenomenon could be ascribed to the increase of the interlayer distance induced by the steric hindrance of the functional groups at the edge of graphene or the plane distortion of sp3 carbon in the graphene plane [24]. Two peaks of Y-CDs and R-L-CDs at 26.1° and 26.4° corresponded to the typical graphite structure (26.5°), which indicated that the internal structure of these CDs was mainly formed by the dense accumulation of highly conjugated sp2 domain [16,36]. Importantly, the R-L-CDs possessed the strongest peak at 26.4°, suggesting that the red emission CDs had the highest degree of graphitization compared with B-CDs and Y-CDs [4,15,24,37]. In the typical Raman spectra of the three samples, there were two classical signal bands at 1350 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1, corresponding to the disordered D-band of carbon material and the G-band of graphite (Fig. 3i). The ID/IG ratios of B-CDs, Y-CDs and R-L-CDs were 1.03 0.98 and 0.87, reflecting that the graphitization degree of red emitting CDs was the highest [24]. Therefore, the fluorescent emission wavelength of these CDs shifted red with the enhancement of the graphitization degree of the obtained CDs [4,34]. Then, the functional groups of three CDs were investigated by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). As illustrated in Fig. 3h, the stretching vibrations at 3500 cm−1, 1786 cm−1, 1675 cm−1 and 1530 cm−1 were assigned to the O-H/N-H, C=O, C=N and C=C bonds, confirming the existence of various hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of CDs, which was beneficial to their biocompatibility [16,18]. Moreover, the peaks at 1240 cm−1 and 1100 cm−1 corresponded to C-N and C-O stretching vibrations, respectively [15]. Therefore, these results indicated that citric acid (or its decomposition products) and water or DMF could form CDs with polyaromatic structures through decarboxylation/amidation, polymerization and carbonization [42].

Next, the contribution of different functional groups to fluorescence emission was investigated by regulating CDs surface structure [4,42]. Typically, NaBH4 was applied to reduce aldehydes and ketones to alcohols while NaOH was utilized to control the acidic groups on the surface of CDs [39]. After adding different concentrations of NaBH4 and NaOH to the three CDs solutions, respectively, the fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs decreased and the emission peak positions shifted to some extent compared with B-CDs and R-L-CDs (Fig. S3), which indicated that the surface state of Y-CDs was activated and its fluorescence emission was mainly related to the surface functional groups. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity and peak shift of B-CDs and R-L-CDs did not change significantly, suggesting that the surface groups of these two CDs were inert and their fluorescence emission were mainly related to the internal structure [4,39,42].

Subsequently, the surface analyses of these CDs were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As described in Fig. S4, the full spectra presented that B-CDs, Y-CDs and R-L-CDs were mainly composed of C, N and O elements with the binding energies of C 1s at 285 eV, N 1s at 400 eV and O 1s at 532 eV, which demonstrated that their constituent elements were unanimous [4,38,43]. The high-resolution C1s spectrum was deconvoluted into five peaks, corresponding to sp2 carbons (C=C, 283.80 eV), sp3 carbons (C-N, 284.10 eV) and C-O (285.90 eV), carbonyl carbons (C=O, 286.20 eV) and carboxyl carbons (COOH, 288.50 eV) (Fig. 4 a,4d and 4 g) [11,36]. The carbon atomic ratios of B-CDs, Y-CDs and R-L-CDs were 66.31 %, 69.99 % and 81.23 %, indicating the increase of the carbonization degree (Table S3). Moreover, the B-CDs had lower carbonization degree and higher oxygen element content compared with R-L-CDs (Table S4), which suggested that B-CDs underwent partial carbonization and over-oxidation, resulting in less core carbonation and more surface oxidation [21]. As illustrated in Fig. 4b, 4e and 4 h, the high-resolution N 1s spectrum could be fitted well with three peaks at 398.60 eV, 400.00 eV and 401.30 eV, which were attributed to pyridine, pyrrolic and graphite N [16,42,44]. The relative content of pyridine N could affect the total nitrogen content in these CDs. It could be clearly observed from Table S3 and Table S4 that the content of pyridine N reached the highest value in B-CDs, and the total nitrogen content of Y-CDs and R-L-CDs decreased sequentially [1]. Moreover, the integral area of graphitized N content increased obviously from 7.52 % to 37.00 % (Table S4), suggesting that the graphitization degree was important for the red shift of CDs fluorescence emission [4]. The relative contents of C=C/C-C in R-L-CDs were higher compared with B-CDs and Y-CDs, suggesting that the content of effective π-conjugated framework (sp2) in R-L-CDs (27.20 %) was significantly higher than that in B-CDs (2.46 %) and Y-CDs (12.69 %) (Table S4) [44]. Finally, the high-resolution O 1s could be deconvoluted into two peaks of C=O (531.20 eV) and C-O (532.00 eV), respectively (Fig. 4c, 4f and 4i) [11,32,41,45]. The relative content of C=O also influenced the total O content of these CDs, which increased significantly from 29.38 % of B-CDs to 82.47 % of R-L-CDs, suggesting that the degree of oxidation enhancement from B-CDs to R-CDs (Table S4 and Fig. 4c, 4f and 4i) [1,36]. By combining all these characterization data and analyses, it was believed that the main reason for CDs from blue to red was the enhanced degree of graphitization and the surface state [19]. Besides, the Zeta potential of these CDs in aqueous solution was −11.30 eV, −23.70 eV and −28.40 eV, respectively (Fig. S5), which indicated that three CDs possessed abundant negative charge on surface [44].

Finally, the band gap energy (Eg) was further calculated using the classical formula Eg = 1240/λedge (λedge is the beginning of the first exaction absorption band in the longer wavelength direction) [43,44]. The Eg of the B-CDs (400 nm), Y-CDs (485 nm), and R-L-CDs (625 nm) was calculated to be 3.10 eV, 2.57 eV and 1.98 eV (Fig. 2a-2c), indicating that the red shift of emission wavelength was correlated with the size of band gap [16,43]. Secondly, the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) energy levels of three types of CDs were further investigated by cyclic voltammetry. These energy levels were determined according to the empirical formulas:
(1)
ELUMO = −e (E R
ed + 4.4)
(2)
EHOMO = −e(EOx + 4.4)
(3)
EHOMO = ELUMO−Eg

Among them, EOx and ERed were oxidation and reduction potential, respectively. According to the Eqs. (1) and (2), the EOx and ERed of B-CDs, Y-CDs and R-L-CDs were determined to be −1.11 eV and 2.11 eV, −0.65 eV and 2.01 eV, −0.47 eV and 1.59 eV, corresponded EHOMO and ELUMO were calculated to be −5.50 eV and −2.19 eV, −5.05 eV and −2.81 eV, −4.87 eV and −2.39 eV, respectively (Fig. S6). Therefore, the Eg of the three samples was calculated to be 3.21 eV, 2.66 eV and 1.96 eV through the Eq. (3), which were consistent with the estimated results from UV–vis absorption [4,43]. Lastly, the DFT calculations were applied to further verify the above experimental results (Fig. S7, Tables S5 and S6). As expected, the band gap variation was the most important factor for the red-shift of the emission wavelength.

Taking the R-L-CDs as the further research object, the application in sensing was explored. Firstly, the stability of R-L-CDs was investigated in different conditions. As illustrated in Fig. S8a, the fluorescence intensity declined in the pH value range from 1 to 4 and remained constant in the range of 5 to 13 [40]. This pH-dependent emission phenomenon was mainly related to the protonation and deprotonation of functional groups on the surface of R-L-CDs [41,45,46]. Secondarily, the effects of irradiation time and strong H2O2 on fluorescent performance of R-L-CDs were tested [47]. The fluorescent intensity of R-L-CDs could maintain above 80–85 % after continuous irradiation for 60 min (Fig. S8b), respectively [48]. Notably, the fluorescent intensity hardly changed under a wide range of NaCl concentrations (0–1 M) (Fig. S8c). Moreover, the fluorescence intensity remained stable after co-incubation with 110 μM H2O2 (Fig. S8d). As shown in Fig. S8e, no significant decrease in fluorescent intensity of R-L-CDs was observed between 20 and 80 °C [8]. Finally, the excellent stability of R-L-CDs in various organic solvents (ethanol (EA), N, N, 2-methylformamide (DMF), methanol (MT), cyclohexane (CYH), toluene (TL), acetone (CP)) was dedicated to the formation of stable surface electronic states independent of the dipole moment of the solvent (Fig. S8f) [46]. Therefore, all these results revealed that the as-prepared R-L-CDs had excellent optical stability and colloidal stability under different experimental conditions, which was beneficial to the applications in a complex biological environment [12,48].

L-Asp as a vital neurotransmitter plays an important role for human health [27]. Therefore, accurate tracking of L-Asp in water and other media has become one of the most worrisome challenges [26]. Initially, the selectivity of R-L-CDs in complex biological environments including metal ions (K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Ag+ and Pb2+), small molecules (Glu, Ga, CA, HClO and ·OH) and a variety of amino acids molecules (L-Leu, L-Arg, L-Lys, L-Trp, L-His, L-Val, L-Pro, L-Phe, L-Asp and L-Ile) was explored [41,48,49]. As illustrated in Fig. 5 a and 5b, L-Asp could induce a strong fluorescent quenching, while the effect of other amino acids and reactive oxygen species could be ignored, which indicated that the R-L-CDs could specifically detect L-Asp. Then, the fluorescence intensity of R-L-CDs declined sharply with the increase of L-Asp concentration and the fluorescence intensity was quenched 82 % at the final concentration of 200 μM (Fig. 5d and 5f). Moreover, the fluorescence significantly dimmed and the color also changed from red to light pink under the irradiation of the UV–vis at 365 nm. More importantly, Fig. 5e displayed an excellent linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of R-L-CDs and the concentration of L-Asp in the range of 10–100 μM, and the detection limit (LOD) was calculated to be 80 nM (3σ/s, σ is the standard deviation of 10 blank measurements, and s is the slope of the calibration curve). These results suggested that these R-L-CDs possessed excellent sensitivity and selectivity for the detection of L-Asp [28].

Subsequently, the feasibility of R-L-CDs-L-Asp system to specifically sense L-Lys was investigated [47]. The selectivity of the sensor was evaluated by parallel measurement of the fluorescence response of R-L-CDs-L-Asp system to other amino acids (L-Cys, L-Leu, L-Arg, L-Lys, L-Trp, L-His, Val, L-Pro, L-Phe, L-Asp and L-Ile) and small molecules (CA, Glu, VC, VB12, Ga, Met, Cre and Mel) [34,35]. As described in Fig. 5c, only L-Lys could restore the fluorescence intensity of R-L-CDs at the excitation wavelength of 520 nm. After the addition of various ions and small molecular substances, the response of R-L-CDs-L-Asp system hardly changed, demonstrating that the R-L-CDs had a high selectivity to L-Lys [30,32]. As depicted in Fig. 5g, the fluorescence signal of the R-L-CDs-L-Asp system at 630 nm was restored progressively as the concentration of L-Lys increased to 500 μM, and the color of the solution changed from light pink to red (Fig. 5i). Likewise, there was an excellent linear relationship in the interval 10 to 100 μM as well as the LOD was calculated to be 55.6 nM (Fig. 5h). On the contrary, when D-Asp with the concentration of up to 1 mM was added to R-L-CDs, the fluorescence intensity only decreased by one-third (Fig. S9a). Then, the fluorescence of R-L-CDs could not be effectively restored after the introduction of D-Lys in the composite system (Fig. S9b). Therefore, the fluorescence could not be quenched and recovered by D-Asp and D-Lys sequentially because of the dimensional position and steric hindrance influence [13,50]. In summary, the prepared R-L-CDs could be regarded as a sensitive fluorescent sensor for highly selective and reliable monitoring of L-Asp and L-Lys [32].

Due to the existence of abundant functional groups on the surface of CDs, they could selectively recognize target analytes through multiple mechanisms [51]. Thus, a series of experiments were conducted to reveal the response mechanism of R-L-CDs to L-Asp and L-Lys. First, the fluorescent emission spectrum of R-L-CDs did not cover the absorption spectrum of L-Asp, ruling out the IFE mechanism (Fig. S10) [13]. Second, no new absorption band appeared after the addition of L-Asp, indicating that the fluorescent quenching mode belonged to the process of dynamic quenching due to the electron transfer (Fig. 6 a) [26,27]. Third, the time-resolved fluorescence (TRPL) decays curves of R-L-CDs and L-Asp were measured by fluorescent spectroscopy [4,25]. An apparent decline from 1.05 ns to 0.03 ns was observed in the fluorescent lifetime decay curves in the presence of L-Asp (Fig. 6b), which suggested that the quenching type was dynamic process [13,51]. Subsequently, the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of R-L-CDs and L-Asp were calculated by DFT [43,48]. The DFT simulation results indicated that the electrons on the LUMO orbital of the R-L-CDs could successfully transfer to the LUMO orbital of L-Asp (Fig. 6d and Fig. S11), resulting in fluorescent quenching. Most importantly, these simulation results were further demonstrated by cyclic voltammetry (Fig. 6e). And the LUMO orbital energy levels of R-L-CDs and L-Asp were calculated as −3.80 eV and −4.01 eV according to formulas (1) and (2). Additionally, the optical energy bandgap (Eg from the absorption edge in the absorption spectrum) of R-L-CDs and L-Asp were estimated to be 2.40 eV and 1.70 eV according to the formula (3). Thus, the electrons in the excited state of R-L-CDs had an opportunity to transfer to the orbital of L-Asp, leading to fluorescence quenching (Fig. 6f) [51]. Therefore, the R-L-CDs displayed outstanding selectivity towards L-Asp by electron transfer process [37].

Moreover, the fluorescent recovery mechanism of R-L-CDs-L-Asp after adding L-Lys was investigated. On the one hand, the Zeta-potential was applied to explain the magnitude of electrostatic charge on surface of R-L-CDs. The microscopic charge state of R-L-CDs was −28.40 mV, indicating that R-L-CDs had the excellent stability. In the presence of L-Asp, the charge shifted towards the positive charge (-18.90 mV) and the colloid stability of R-L-CDs decreased (Fig. 6c). The total potential was −35.30 mV when L-Lys was introduced into R-L-CDs-Asp system, which was more than the mixed solution of R-L-CDs and L-Asp, leading to the rapid recovery of fluorescence [18]. According to the reported, the proteins, organic materials, polymers or silica could enhance the fluorescence intensity of CDs by surface passivation, and L-Lys could also passivate the BSA-coated CDs surface and increase the fluorescence intensity [32,52]. Therefore, the enhancement of fluorescence by L-Lys might be due to surface passivation. And this inference was confirmed by recording the UV–vis absorption spectra of R-L-CDs before and after L-Lys addition (Fig. 6a), which illustrated no significant difference in the main absorption peaks. In addition, the addition of L-Lys increased the absorbance in the range of 250 nm to 400 nm, which could prove the occurrence of passivation. In other hands, the L-Lys was present as a positive ion in aqueous solutions and the amine of L-Lys was protonated to –NH3 +. However, electron donors (such as hydroxyl and amine) in R-L-CDs were more likely to adsorb the protonated amines. Therefore, the protonated amines on the L-Lys surface interacted with the R-L-CDs to restore the fluorescence through electrostatic attraction and hydrogen bonding [31]. Subsequently, the energy of the entire R-L-CDs-L-Asp-L-Lys system declined owing to the π → π* conjugation and p → π* conjugation of the complex, and the electronic transition of π → π* and n → π* more easily achieved [8,41]. Thus, the UV–vis absorption of R-L-CDs was enhanced in the presence of L-Lys as well as the fluorescence was rapidly restored (Fig. 6a).

To verify the reliability of the chiral fluorescent sensor, the real samples including human urine and mouse serum were selected as the model matrix for measuring L-Asp and L-Lys [25]. The collected urine and serum samples were diluted 30 times and 100 times with ultrapure water for further analysis, respectively [27,45]. Various levels (50, 100 and 200 μM) of L-Asp were added, followed by increasing concentrations of L-Lys, and the fluorescence responses of the probes at 630 nm for all these samples were measured. The recovery rates of L-Asp and L-Lys ranged from 85.32 % to 117.19 % and 86.39 % to 101.00 % and the RSD was below 3.00 % (Table S7). Therefore, the R-L-CDs could effectively shield strong background interference from complex samples and had great application prospects in monitoring L-Asp and L-Lys in actual biological samples analysis [10,41,42].

Prior to investigate the potential of CDs for imaging and sensing in living cells, the toxicity of these CDs to HeLa cells was measured using MTT assay [19,25,34,35,53]. As shown in Figs. S12a-S12c, HeLa cells exhibited satisfactory cell viability after 24 h and 48 h co-incubation with high concentration of CDs (1000 μg/mL), respectively. Moreover, the 4T1 cells had also been applied to assess cytotoxicity. The cell survival rate was higher than 85 % after these cells were incubated for 48 h (Fig. S12a-12c), which suggested that the excellent biocompatibility of these CDs [17,18]. Therefore, the low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility of these CDs provided the possibilities for intracellular sensing and imaging [10,17].

Inspired by the high photostability and extremely low cytotoxicity of these CDs, the cell imaging and sensing experiments were conducted [47,48,53]. As shown in Fig. 7
a, the cells possessed intact morphology and exhibited three colors at different excitation wavelengths after the 4T1 cells were incubated with B-CDs, Y-CDs and R-L-CDs (100 µg·mL−1) for 4 h [54]. Moreover, the fluorescence in 4T1 cells was mostly distributed in the cytoplasm, indicating that the as-prepared CDs could enter cells successfully through endocytosis due to their small particle size and good biocompatibility [21,41]. Then, the cells were treated with Hoechst33342 solution for co-localization experiments. As described in Fig. 7c, Hoechst33342 stained the nucleus well in the blue channel, while R-L-CDs were observed only in the cell cytoplasm in the red channel, suggesting that R-L-CDs could not target the nucleus [10,25,53]. Subsequently, confocal fluorescence images of R-L-CDs co-incubated with 4T1 cells in the presence of different concentrations of L-Asp and L-Lys were collected, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 7b, the intensity of R-L-CDs was gradually quenched with the increase of the concentration of L-Asp from 0 to 200 μM. And it could be effectively recovered when 500 μM L-Lys was introduced into the R-L-CDs-L-Asp system. Therefore, these results demonstrated that R-L-CDs could be applied as an efficient fluorescent probe for the rapid detection of L-Asp and L-Lys in living cells [13,19].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone application for the detection of L-Asp and L-Lys
ce:section.ce:para: Recently, the portable detection of analytes was still limited by the complex operation and the unpopularity of laboratory instruments [55]. Therefore, it was necessary to establish comprehensive and manually carried quantitative analysis equipment, where CDs wrapped in a solid support, such as paper-based sensors, have received much attention in field detection [56]. We designed a convenient paper-based CD sensor to quickly detect the samples with the help of the smartphone platform. As illustrated in Fig. 8a, the fluorescent probe exhibited a continuous color change from red to colorless under 365 nm when the L-Asp concentration increased from 0 to 1500 μM [48]. On the contrary, the fluorescence intensity was gradually enhanced from colorless to pink when the incremental concentrations of L-Lys were mixed to the R-L-CDs-Asp system. Meanwhile, the paper based sensor showed the same phenomenon in the presence of analyte (Fig. 8b). However, it was difficult to directly determine the actual content of the measured object because the subtle color changes were not easily observed by the naked eye [56,57]. To solve this problem, a smartphone sensor platform was designed to analyze color parameters of the paper during real-time detection. And the high-resolution camera of smartphone and third-party software was applied as results analysis tools. Moreover, the RGB values and Lab values corresponding to these photos were obtained through the color recognizer for further statistical analysis [56]. The color data were shown in Tables S8 and S9. The variation of L, a, b and R, G, B values with the concentration of L-Asp and L-Lys measured from the paper strip was displayed in Fig. S13a-12d. The L values decreased with the increase of concentration of L-Asp as well as the value remained fairly stable (L represents brightness, a represents the color range from magenta to green and b represents the color range from yellow to blue). In addition, the R values decreased with the increase of concentration of L-Asp while the B and G value was relatively stable. The calculation displayed a linear relationship between L + a + b and L-Asp and L-Lys, respectively. Photographs of paper strips and solutions containing L-Asp and L-Lys were collected, and the calibration equation was established (Fig. 8c and 8d) [55-57]. For the paper strips, the L + a + b value was inversely proportional to the concentrations of L-Asp, and the solution color gradually changed from red to deep-blue. There was an excellent linear relationship between the L + a + b value and the L-Asp at a range of 0–1500 μM and could be expressed as y = -0.128x + 105.56 (R2 = 0.994). Then, the value of L + a + b increased with increasing concentration of L-Lys (0–1500 μM) and an excellent liner relationship could be expressed as y = 0.128x + 22.10 (R2 = 0.995), and the color of the paper strip gradually changed from blue to pink. In a word, the results demonstrated that the smartphone-assisted fluorescence sensing platform had a fast detection speed, convenient reading and intelligent [55,56].

In summary, the multi-color fluorescent CDs were prepared via a one-step solvothermal method using CA and urea as carbon and nitrogen source. The as-prepared CDs exhibited stable, bright and controllable fluorescence at different excitation wavelengths. The detailed characterization and analysis disclosed that the dehydration and carbonization processes of different reaction solvents and raw materials decided the content of sp2-conjugated and sp3-conjugated domains, and finally produced different emission colors from blue to red. Moreover, the three CDs had low cytotoxicity and excellent optical stability and could be applied in vitro biological imaging. More importantly, the R-L-CDs as a sensitive fluorescent probe could selectively respond to L-Asp and L-Lys in aqueous solution and in living cells. In addition, a smart phone sensor platform was designed for real-time detection and analysis of paper color parameters. Therefore, this work provided not only a simple approach to obtain three CDs with excellent optical characteristics by controlling the formation process, but also a new technical approach of the identification of amino acid enantiomers for the early prevention of amino acid metabolism diseases [20].
